generations.text:
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the sea roars with a fury that has long since passed the point of reason. I write this with trembling hands, for I fear that the storm may claim us both before the dawn.

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice. I have tried to forget the last time we saw each other, the way your eyes turned cold and distant, as if I were a stranger in a strange land. I have tried to write to you, but my heart has been too heavy, and my pen too clumsy. I fear that even if I did write, you might not wish to read it.

I know that you have your own life now, and that I have no right to intrude upon it. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I have seen many storms, but none have ever felt as cruel as the one that has been placed upon my soul. I have tried to be the father you needed, but I fear I have failed you, and I do not know how to make it right.

Tonight, the winds are rising, and the waves are growing taller. The sea is restless, and I can feel the weight of its anger pressing against the cliffs. I have

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of receiving a reply, but with the weight of a heart that has long since grown heavy from silence. Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on, I have not forgotten the warmth of your presence, the sound of your laughter echoing through these stone walls. You were my light, Eleanor—my guiding star, even in the darkest of nights.

Tonight, the sky is dark, and the sea roars like a beast. The wind howls through the cracks in the lighthouse, and I can feel the storm gathering strength. I have not seen the horizon in days, and I fear the waves may soon reach the cliffs. I have always believed that the sea is a faithful companion, but tonight, I fear it may not be so kind.

You were always so full of fire and spirit, Eleanor. You wanted more from life than I could offer. I was a man of duty, bound to this tower, to the sea, to the light. I was not the kind of father you needed. I was not the one who could take you away from this place, who could give you the world you dreamed of. And so you left. You left with your dreams, your ambitions, and your heart.

I have tried to forget the pain of your departure, but it

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the strength to hold a quill with any grace, and the paper is damp with the sea air that seeps through the cracks in my window. The storm is coming—those black clouds that have been gathering for days now, like a gathering of vultures over a corpse. I have seen the sky, Eleanor, and it does not look kind.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and yet the weight of your absence has never lifted. I have kept the lighthouse lit, as I always have, but I have not kept the light of my soul. You were the only thing that ever made me feel like a man worth something. And now, I fear, I am nothing but a shadow of the man I once was.

I do not know what has become of you. I have heard nothing but the howling wind and the crashing of waves. I have tried to write, but my hands have trembled too much, and my heart has been too heavy. Perhaps you have forgotten me, as I have forgotten how to speak your name without a lump in my throat.

But tonight, as the storm approaches, I feel the need to say this: I have always loved you, Eleanor. I have always been here, waiting for you to come back. I have kept the lamp burning for you, even when I did not believe you would return. And

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. The sea has been a faithful companion, but it has not been able to carry my words to you. Tonight, as the wind howls like a vengeful spirit and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury I have never known, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever.

I have always believed that the lighthouse is a place of guidance, a beacon for those lost at sea. But perhaps I have been blind to the storms within myself. You were my light, Eliza, and I let it fade. I let the years and the distance grow between us, and I have never found the strength to reach for you again. I have spent these years watching the horizon, hoping that one day you might return, but I have never dared to reach out.

I know you must think of me as a man who has failed you, a father who turned his back on his own flesh. I do not deny that. I have been a fool, and I have been cruel. I have let pride and fear keep me from the one thing that truly matters—family. But I write this not to beg your forgiveness, for I do not deserve it, but to tell you the truth. I have always loved you,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not seen the light of day in days, and I fear I may never see it again. But I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall lose myself entirely.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, cruel years since the last time you set foot in this lighthouse. You left like a storm, and I have been left to weather the silence in its wake.

I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you will ever forgive me. But I must say it, for the wind is rising, and I may not have the strength to speak when the time comes.

You were always too bright, too fierce for this world. You had the fire of a thousand suns, and I feared that fire would one day consume me. I told you, in my own way, that this life was not for you. That this lighthouse, this lonely duty, this endless watch over the sea—was not the life you were meant to live. And you left, as you always did, with your head held high and your heart locked behind a door I could not open.

I have not forgotten the last time we spoke. You stood at the door, your eyes like

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1889**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea, which has been my companion for thirty years, now roars with a fury I have never known. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I must say what I have long been unable to.

You were twelve when you left this house, Eliza. I remember the day like it was yesterday—your eyes filled with fire, your voice sharp with defiance. “You never listen to me, Father,” you said. And I did not. I was too busy tending to the light, too wrapped up in my duties, too blind to the pain I caused you. I was a fool, and I have been a fool ever since.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a single letter, and I know I have no right to expect you to forgive me. But tonight, as I stand before the great beacon that has guided countless ships through the darkest nights, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever. You were right, Eliza. I was a terrible father, and I have been a terrible man.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may claim me. I have

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the wind that howls against the cliffs, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you left this place, and though the lighthouse stands firm as ever, I have not. Not in the way you might expect.

I have not spoken to you, not because I do not wish to, but because the words would not come. And now, as the storm gathers, I find myself in a state of quiet desperation. The sea is rising, the sky is black as ink, and the wind sings a dirge that echoes in my bones. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

You were a child when you left, and I thought I could keep you safe, even from yourself. I told you I loved you, and you said you did not need me. I did not understand then, and I do not now. But I do know this: I have spent every night since you left sitting in this tower, watching the world below, wondering if I had done something to make you turn your back on me.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light of your laughter, your questions, your stubbornness. I would have given you the world, and you gave me nothing but silence. I have tried to forgive you, but I find I cannot. Not yet.

If I do not survive this night,

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now, not with the calm of a clear night, but with the weight of a storm upon the horizon—both in the sky and in my heart. The wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars with a fury that threatens to swallow us whole. I do not know if I shall see another dawn, and if I do, I do not know if I shall see you again.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and in that time, the world has turned, and I have remained. I have tended the light, kept the fire burning, and watched the waves crash against the rocks with a steadfastness that has, I fear, become my only companion. I have not written to you, nor have I sent a word, for I did not know what to say. And perhaps I still do not.

You were young when you left, and I was a fool to let you go. I thought I was protecting you, that the world beyond this rock was too cruel, too dangerous for a girl of your tender years. But I was wrong. I was blind to the truth, and I have paid the price for my ignorance. You grew up, you found your way, and I remained behind, a man frozen in time, bound to the lighthouse, and to my regrets.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent together, when you were small and I would read

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may not spare us. I have not spoken to you in five years, and though the years have worn me down, I cannot help but think of you now more than ever.

You were once my light, my guiding star in the darkest nights. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, eyes filled with a sorrow I could not then understand. I was a fool, Eleanor. I let pride and silence drive a wedge between us. I did not know how to say the words, how to ask for forgiveness. I thought time would mend what was broken, but it only deepened the wound.

I have spent these years keeping the light burning, not just for the sailors who pass by, but for you. Each time I climbed the spiral stairs, I thought of you. Each time I turned the lens, I felt your presence in the glow. You were always in my thoughts, even when I tried to forget.

Tonight, the storm is fierce. The sea roars like a beast, and the wind is a relentless force. I fear I may not make it through this night. But even in my darkest hour, I find comfort in knowing that you are still my daughter, and that I have always loved you, even when I failed you

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the narrow windows of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, the sky a swirling tempest of black and silver, and I fear I may not see another dawn. But I must write, for you—though it may be the last thing I ever do.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. The silence between us has grown heavier than the sea, and I have long since ceased to wonder if I have done right. You were once my greatest joy, my light in the darkest hours, and now I am left in the dark, both literally and in my heart.

I have always been a man of routine, of order, of duty. The lighthouse was my life, my purpose. I tended the flame, kept the light burning, and watched over the ships that passed. But now, the flame flickers, and I fear it may go out before I can tend it once more.

I have not seen you in five years, not even a letter. I have tried to write, but the words would not come. I have tried to call, but the sea would not carry my voice. And so, I have been left with only the wind and the waves, and the memory of your laughter.

You were never meant to leave, Eleanor. You were born to the light, just as I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long, bitter winters since I last set pen to paper, and I fear the storm that now churns beyond the cliffs may be the final one I shall weather. Yet, even in this hour of uncertainty, I am compelled to speak, for I have never ceased to think of you.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a single letter, for I feared the words I might utter would do more harm than good. You were young when you left, and I was old, stubborn, and blind to the pain I caused. I have often wondered whether I was a father or a tyrant, and in the quiet hours, I have come to believe it was both.

You were not the first to leave me, but you were the first to leave with the fire of youth and the promise of a better world. I did not understand then, and I do not understand now, how a child could so easily abandon the hearth of her father. But I do understand this: you were right to go. I was wrong to hold you back.

The storm that now gathers in the east is unlike any we have seen in memory. The sea roars like a beast, and the winds howl with the fury of the damned. I have lit the lantern, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray that the light may

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heavy heart, my hands trembling not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with fury. I fear the storm may claim me before dawn, and I would have one last word from my own blood before the world takes me.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, and yet I carry you with me in every flicker of the beacon, in every glance at the horizon. You were but a child when you left, and I, a man too proud to beg for your return. I told myself that distance would mend what pride had broken, but it has only left me with silence and the hollow echo of your footsteps.

I have always been the keeper of this light, and in that role, I have watched countless ships find their way home, even when the sea seemed determined to drown them. But I have never been able to guide you back, my dear. You were the only one I ever truly wanted to return to, and I have been a fool to let pride and fear keep me from you.

If I do not survive this storm, know that I have not died in vain. I have done my duty, and I have kept the light burning for all who sail these waters. But I would

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in some distant land, far from the salt-laden winds and the ceaseless roar of the sea that has been my constant companion for these many years. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often found myself wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has faded like the last embers of a dying fire.

I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling our bond, but with the weight of a storm that looms on the horizon. The skies have darkened with an ominous promise, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now churns with fury. I have seen such storms before, and I know the dangers they bring. I pray that you are safe, and that you have found peace in the world beyond this lighthouse.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and I fear that the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing year. There are moments, as I sit by the light, when I find myself longing for your laughter, for the warmth of your presence, for the love that once bound us so tightly. I have tried to keep the light burning, not just for the ships that pass by, but for the hope that one day, we might find our way back to each other.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1898**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I know it may not. I have written many letters before this one—some sent, some left unsealed in the drawer of my desk, others burned in the hearth. But this one, I feel, must reach you. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that time may soon be up.

You were once the light of my life, the only child I had, and the only one who ever truly understood the loneliness of this life on the cliffs. I remember the days when you would come to visit, and we would sit by the fire, talking long into the night. You used to ask me about the sea, about the stars, about the storms that would come unbidden in the dead of night. I used to tell you that the sea is a fickle mistress, and that the lighthouse is her only servant, always watching, always waiting.

But that was before you left. I don’t know what drove you away, or what made you choose the city over the sea, the life of a woman over the life of a man who could only ever be a keeper. I have never forgiven myself for not standing up to your father, for not standing up for you. I should have known better than to let him take you away. I should have known

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart that has long since grown cold, yet still beats for you. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and in that time, the world has turned, the tides have shifted, and the sea has grown more restless than ever. Tonight, a storm unlike any other is gathering on the horizon, and I fear it may be the end of me.

You were always a storm in your own right, my child. Wild, untamed, and full of a fire that could not be tamed. I tried to guide you, to steer you toward the shore, but you always chose the tempest. I remember the day you left—your eyes were full of fury, your voice like thunder. You called me a fool, a man who saw only the light and not the darkness within. And I let you go. I did not stop you, though I should have. I did not plead, though I would have given anything to have you stay.

But I have learned, in these years of solitude, that some things cannot be undone. Some things must be let go. And so I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what is broken, but with the hope that you might understand.

You were never the daughter I wanted. You were never the daughter I deserved. But you were always my daughter, and I have always loved you, even when you

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the world has turned, I fear it has turned away from you. I have not seen your face in the mirror since that day you left, and I have not heard your voice since the night you walked out of our home.

The sea has always been a cruel mistress, but tonight, it seems to whisper secrets that I cannot bear to hear. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn. I have spent my life standing at this lighthouse, guiding ships through the darkest nights, but now I find myself lost in the darkness of my own soul.

I have no words to explain the years that have passed, nor the pain of your absence. I have tried to be a father, a husband, a man of faith, but I have failed you. I have failed to understand the fire that burned within you, the anger, the sorrow, the longing that I could not reach. I have been a fool, Clara, and I have not been worthy of your love.

But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast and the waves rise like the wrath of the gods, I find myself thinking of you. I have spent my life watching the sea, and now I see it as I have never seen it before. It is not just a force of nature; it

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast at my door. The sea rages, the sky is split in two by lightning, and I fear I may not see dawn. But if I do, I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have no one else to tell the truth.

You were not the first to leave me, but you were the last, and I have not yet buried you.

I have not written to you because I did not know where you were, and I have not sought you out because I feared what I might find. You were always so... *distant*, Eleanor. Even as a child, you had this way of looking at the world as if it were a book you had already read and found the ending unsatisfactory.

But I have missed you, child. More than I have ever admitted. I have missed your laughter, your stubbornness, your *eyes*—those same grey eyes that stared back at me from the mirror every morning, as if I had not been the one who had raised you.

You were never meant to leave. I made a mistake when I told you to go. I thought I was protecting you. That the sea was too dangerous, that the lighthouse was too lonely, that the world was too cruel. But I was wrong, Eleanor. And I have spent five long years regretting it.

I write to you now, with the sea howling like a wounded beast at my window, and the wind tearing at the shutters with a fury that makes me think the very heavens are in revolt. It is the kind of night that stirs the soul with both dread and longing, and I find myself with more to say than I ever thought I would to you.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, since the day you left this house, like a shadow cast by the sun. I have not spoken to you, nor have I tried. Not out of spite, no—though I have felt it. But out of a kind of numbness, a quiet sorrow that has settled in my bones like the salt of the sea.

I have been the keeper of this lighthouse for many years now, and I have seen countless storms. I have stood upon these cliffs, alone, with only the light to guide the lost and the weary. But tonight, I fear this storm may be unlike any other. The winds are howling with a voice that seems to echo with the past, and the sea is rising, black and angry, as though it wishes to swallow the world whole.

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with a quill that trembles, not from the wind that howls outside, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since we last exchanged a word, and though time has worn the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the ache of your absence. I have kept the lighthouse as one keeps a wound—clean, silent, and ever watchful.  

Tonight, a storm is coming, a tempest of such fury that even the sea itself seems to hold its breath before it breaks. The sky is a canvas of iron and flame, and I have seen the sea rise like a beast in wrath. I do not know if I shall see dawn. And if I do not, I wish, with all the strength I have left, that you might hear this.  

You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. You were the reason I rose with the sun and stood watch through the darkest hours. I remember the day you left—your eyes like distant stars, your voice like a whisper carried on the wind. You said I was too stern, too rigid, too much a man of stone. I did not argue. I did not plead. I simply watched the waves, knowing that you would not stay.  

But I have not forgotten. I have not forgiven. And I have not stopped hoping.  

There is something I have never told you, not even to myself.

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. I do not know if I shall see dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not be able to speak to you again. But if I can, I must.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, and I have tried, in the quiet hours of the night, to imagine what you might look like now. I imagine you are grown, perhaps even married, with children of your own. I imagine you are far from this lonely island, far from the sea, far from me.

I have kept the light burning for you, Eliza. Every night, I have stood at the top of the tower and turned the great lamp, not only for the ships that sail the waters, but for you. I have thought of you as I watched the stars, and I have prayed that one day, you would come back to see this place, to see the light that has guided me through so many years of solitude.

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have known, and I fear it may claim me before the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and in that time, I have grown accustomed to the silence of this lighthouse. The light still shines, as it always has, but it no longer feels the same. It no longer carries the weight of your laughter or the warmth of your presence. I have kept the light burning, not just for the ships that pass by, but for the memory of you.

**To My Beloved Daughter,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may not. The time has come for me to write to you, though I must confess I have not held a pen in my hand in quite some time. The weight of five years of silence has pressed heavily upon my heart, and the storm that looms on the horizon has stirred within me a longing I had long thought buried.

You may well have forgotten me by now, or perhaps you have chosen to do so. I understand that time is a cruel master, and that we are all shaped by the choices we make. I have made mine, and I have lived with the consequences. But tonight, as I prepare for the storm, I find myself reflecting on the days when your laughter echoed through this lighthouse, and the nights when we shared stories by the flickering light of the beacon.

I have always believed in the power of the light, not just as a guide for sailors, but as a symbol of hope and resilience. And yet, even the strongest light can be extinguished by the fiercest winds. I fear that I may not be able to keep the light burning for much longer. The storm is not merely a tempest of wind and rain; it is a test of my resolve, and I am not sure I will emerge from it unscathed.

I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to forget, to forgive, to move on—but the sea has a way of reminding me of the things I cannot leave behind.

Tonight, the storm comes. The sky is black, the wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with fury. I have stood on the cliffs for hours, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and I have felt the weight of the world pressing down on my shoulders. I do not know if I will see dawn. The lighthouse is my home, my duty, my life—but I am not sure this time, I will be here to greet it.

You must understand, Eleanor, that I have not written to you because I have not had the strength to. The burden of your anger, your silence, your absence—it has been a weight I have carried alone. I have not spoken of you in these years, not even to the other keepers. I have kept my heart locked in a box, buried beneath the salt and the sand.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink smudged from my trembling hands and the wind howling like a beast outside these walls. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear the storm may claim more than the lighthouse. But if I should fall, I hope these words find you—though I know not how.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of watching the waves crash against the rocks, and of wondering if you would ever return. I have not spoken of you to any soul, not even to the priest who comes once a month. I have kept your name in my heart, though I have not kept your love.

You were a spirited child, full of questions and fire. I used to tell you stories of the sea, of how it was both a friend and a foe. You would press your ear to the glass of the lighthouse and listen for the whispers of the wind. I used to think you would grow up to be a woman of courage, like your mother. But you chose your own path, and I did not follow.

I have no regrets, not truly. I have done my duty, as you once told me I would. The lighthouse stands, and the light burns. I have tried to reach you, to understand, to forgive. But the silence between us has grown into a chasm so deep, I fear it may never be bridged.

I have always been a man of the sea, Eleanor. My life has been dictated by its tides, its moods, its unrelenting power. You, however, were never meant for the sea. You were meant for the land, for the warmth of hearths, for the comfort of home. And yet, you chose to walk away. I do not know why. Perhaps you believed I had no place in your life. Perhaps you thought I had never loved you as you deserved. Perhaps you were right.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that now brews on the horizon. The sea roars like a beast in torment, and the wind howls as though the very heavens are weeping. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and I fear that this may be the last letter I ever write.

You were but a child when you left, and I, in my folly, let pride and silence drive you away. I should have held you closer, spoken to you more, understood the pain that you carried. I should have known that the world was too cruel, too cold, for a girl like you.

I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you still care. But I must say it, for I have never had the courage to speak these words aloud.

You were my light, Eliza. Not the light of the lighthouse, though that is what I was sworn to protect. No, you were the light in my life—your laughter, your dreams, the way you looked at the stars as though they were your own. I watched you grow, and though I did not always understand you, I loved you all the same.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1897**

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may not bring you much comfort. I write to you now, not with the ease of a man who has long since laid down his pen, but with the urgency of a man who knows the storm is coming—and who is not certain he will see the dawn.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I confess I have not forgotten the words you left me that night. "You are not the father I wanted," you said. I have heard those words in my sleep, and I have heard them in the howling winds that sweep across this island. They haunt me more than the sea itself.

I have been the lighthouse keeper here for nearly two decades, and I have seen many storms. But this one—this one is different. The sky is black as pitch, and the sea roars as if it seeks to swallow the very land. I have lit the beacon for hours, though I fear it may not be enough. The lantern is old, and the lamp is weak. I have not spoken to a soul in days, save for the gulls that circle the cliffs and the wind that whips through the cracks in the stone.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the world has turned, and the tides have risen, I find myself unable to rest, for the storm that approaches brings with it not only the threat of the sea but the weight of my own mortality.

You were once my light, my greatest pride. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes held a fire that I could not quell. You said I was too strict, too distant. And perhaps I was. I was a man bound to duty, to the lighthouse, to the sea that has been both my life and my curse. I did not know how to be the father you needed, nor did I know how to hold you in my arms without letting go.

But now, as I stand at the edge of the cliff, the wind howling like a banshee, I am reminded of the nights I once spent watching the stars with you. You used to ask me stories of the sea, of the sailors who came and went, of the ghosts that whispered in the fog. I would tell you of the storms that tested even the strongest of men, and how the light of the lighthouse was our salvation.
